Summary: Systematic elaboration. FOXA1 encodes a pioneer factor in the prostate that induces open chromatin to allow transcription factor binding. FOXA1 interacts directly with the Androgen Receptor and helps shape AR signaling that drives growth and survival of normal prostate and prostate cancer cells. FOXA1 also has an AR-independent role in regulating epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition. FOXA1 activity can be modulated post-translationally through mechanisms such as LSD1-mediated protein demethylation. In prostate cancer, mutations in the FOXA1 coding sequence and in cis-regulatory elements lead to functional alterations. Forkhead Wing2 coding mutations emerge in localized disease and truncation mutations with structural variants are enriched in metastatic disease. Indels in the FOXA1 3′ UTR are the dominant alteration type and can be found early in prostate tumours; these indels are absent from bladder cancer. FOXA1 mutations are detected in plasma cell-free DNA from patients with mCRPC. Liquid biopsy tracking permits longitudinal monitoring of minimal residual disease and may inform prognosis and mutant-specific inhibitor development. FOXA1 coding mutations have been reported in up to 9% of primary cancers and in 13% of mCRPC cases. In one NEPC study SNVs mapped to FOXA1 in about 25% of tumours. The Chinese cohort reported FOXA1 mutation in 41% of localized tumours.
References: Teng, M., Zhou, S., Cai, C., Lupien, M., & He, H. H. (2021). Pioneer of prostate cancer: past, present and the future of FOXA1. Protein Cell, 12(1), 29-38. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13238-020-00786-8
